column1: Comprehensive Timeline of Avestan and Related Ancient Languages

column4: Event/Development
Proto-Indo-European language spoken; ancestral to all Indo-European languages including later Avestan and Greek
Proto-Canaanite language emerging from Proto-Afroasiatic; ancestral to later Hebrew, Phoenician, and other Canaanite languages
Proto-Indo-European begins diverging into distinct branches
Early Canaanite dialects developing; earliest precursors to Hebrew emerging
Split of Proto-Indo-Iranian from other Indo-European branches
Proto-Greek developing from Indo-European, ancestor of Mycenaean Greek that would later use Linear B
Development of Proto-Sinaitic script, ancestor of Phoenician and Hebrew alphabets
Split between Proto-Iranian and Proto-Indo-Aryan (ancestor of Sanskrit)
Early Aramaic emerging as a distinct Northwest Semitic language
Development of Proto-Iranian dialects including the ancestor of Avestan
Development of Linear A (undeciphered) and early forms of writing preceding Linear B
Linear B script developed to write Mycenaean Greek, earliest attested form of Greek
Old Avestan period begins; closely related linguistically to Vedic Sanskrit
Composition of early Rigveda hymns; shows strong linguistic similarities with Old Avestan
Proto-Canaanite script evolving toward Phoenician alphabet
Fall of Mycenaean palaces; Linear B script falls out of use; Greek enters "dark age"
Emergence of distinct Phoenician and Hebrew languages from Canaanite; early Hebrew writing
Phoenician alphabet fully developed; adapted for early Hebrew writing
Early Biblical Hebrew period; begins to be written in a variant of the Phoenician script
Approximate time of Zarathustra according to modern scholarship; composition of Gathas in Old Avestan
Aramaic begins spreading as a trade language
Young Avestan period; composition of Yashts, Vendidad and other ritual texts
Greek alphabet developed from Phoenician script; emergence of Archaic Greek dialects
Classical Biblical Hebrew period; standardization of Hebrew writing
Aramaic becomes widespread diplomatic language throughout the Near East
Young Avestan shows morphological and syntactical similarities with Old Persian
Judean exile; increasing Aramaic influence on Hebrew
Traditional (now considered inaccurate) dating of Zarathustra's lifetime
Avestan becomes primarily liturgical; shift of political power to Western Iran; Imperial Aramaic becomes official administrative language
Late Biblical Hebrew period; Aramaic replaces Hebrew as common spoken language among Jews
Hellenistic period; continued oral transmission of Avestan texts; Aramaic remains important administrative language
Septuagint (Greek translation of Hebrew Bible) created; shows Hebrew's evolving status
Avestan preserved as liturgical language among Zoroastrian communities
Mishnaic Hebrew develops; Dead Sea Scrolls written in Hebrew, Aramaic, and Greek
Koine Greek becomes dominant literary language; Hebrew primarily liturgical; Aramaic dialects flourish
Formalization of Zoroastrianism as state religion; Aramaic script adapted for writing Middle Persian (Pahlavi)
Palestinian and Babylonian Talmuds compiled in Aramaic with Hebrew elements
Development of the specialized Avestan alphabet (Din dabireh - "religion writing") derived from Aramaic script
Avestan corpus committed to writing after centuries of oral transmission; creation of the Sasanian Avesta
Avestan no longer fully understood by Zoroastrian commentators, requiring Middle Persian commentaries (Zend)
Classical Arabic becomes dominant; Hebrew preserved as liturgical language; many Aramaic dialects decline
Continued preservation of Avestan texts by Zoroastrian communities under Islamic rule
Medieval Jewish Aramaic dialects flourish in scholarly contexts; Hebrew undergoes revival as literary language
Period after which substantial portions of Avestan literature were lost
Aramaic gradually replaced by Arabic in most regions; surviving mainly in isolated communities
First transcription of Avestan in Brahmi-based scripts by Parsi scholars like Neryosang Dhaval
